notes:
những means “some” or marks a plural noun in a general way. It tells you there’s more than one, without being specific.

It goes before a classifier + noun or directly before some nouns.  

It’s often used when introducing plural things in a general sense. Compared to các, những feels less formal and a bit more descriptive, while các is more neutral and systematic (like listing categories).

You can think of it as a soft plural marker — not exact in number, just “multiple.”
sentenceBreakdowns: Em (I) + thấy (see) + những (some) + con (classifier) + chó (dogs) + nhỏ (small)
Anh (he) + mua (buys) + những (some) + quả (classifier) + táo (apples) + đỏ (red)
Chị (she) + đọc (reads) + những (some) + quyển (classifier) + sách (books) + mới (new)

content.sentence: Tôi muốn tìm ___ cuốn sách hay.

translation.en: I want to find those good books.

exampleSentences: Em thấy những con chó nhỏ.
Anh mua những quả táo đỏ.
Chị đọc những quyển sách mới.

definitions.vi: Một từ chỉ số nhiều được sử dụng trước danh từ trong tiếng Việt.
definitions.en: A plural marker used before nouns in Vietnamese.
definitions.es: Un marcador plural que se usa antes de los sustantivos en vietnamita.

example.vi: Những người bạn của tôi rất tốt.

content.sentence: Tôi đã gặp ___ người bạn cũ.

notes_md:
**những** means “some” or marks a plural noun in a general way. It tells you there’s more than one, without being specific.

It goes **before a classifier + noun** or directly before some nouns.  

It’s often used when introducing plural things in a general sense. Compared to **các**, *những* feels less formal and a bit more descriptive, while *các* is more neutral and systematic (like listing categories).

You can think of it as a soft plural marker — not exact in number, just “multiple.”
